title: Inga edwallii
taxonomy: Inga edwallii belongs to the family Leguminosae, also known as Fabaceae. This classification is due to the plant's characteristics, such as its compound leaves and bean-like pods, which are typical of plants within the Leguminosae family. Inga edwallii is further classified within the subfamily Caesalpinioideae and the tribe Ingeae. The tribe Ingeae is a group of flowering plants that are native to Central and South America, and Inga edwallii is likely part of this group. Within the tribe Ingeae, Inga edwallii is also classified as a species of the Inga genus.
uses:
Inga edwallii has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its anti-inflammatory properties. 
Inga edwallii is said to have been used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. 
Its extracts have been studied for their potential antimicrobial and antioxidant effects. 
The plant's medicinal properties are attributed to the presence of bioactive compounds such as flavonoids and phenolic acids. 
Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of Inga edwallii for medicinal use.

intro: Inga edwallii, also known as Inga edulis, is a species of tree in the family Fabaceae, native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, which include antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties, making it useful for treating various conditions such as fever, cough, and respiratory problems. The bioactive constituents of Inga edwallii include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic compounds, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, Inga edwallii is commonly prepared as a tea or infusion, and also used to make herbal supplements and cough syrups.
